summary: AHIMA supports some deregulatory efforts in HTI-5 but strongly cautions against removing key certification criteria such as C-CDA, patient matching, decision support interventions, and privacy/security requirements. They emphasize the risk to nationwide interoperability, patient safety, and data privacy if these provisions are removed prematurely.
specific_provisions: 45 CFR § 170.315(d) Privacy and Security Certification Criteria
45 CFR § 170.550(h) Privacy and Security Certification Framework
key_arguments: Removing C-CDA certification criteria risks disrupting nationwide interoperability and limits data exchange for under-resourced providers.
Eliminating patient matching requirements jeopardizes patient safety by increasing misidentification risks.
Removing the Decision Support Interventions criterion reduces transparency and trust in AI/DSI tools, hindering innovation adoption.
Dropping privacy and security certification criteria exposes sensitive patient data amid rising cyber threats and increases burden on providers.
recommendations: Retain C-CDA certification criteria until FHIR is fully developed, tested, and widely adopted.
Maintain patient matching requirements or adopt provisions from HR 2002 and the MATCH IT Act to improve patient matching standards.
Preserve the Decision Support Interventions criterion and explore ways to improve it rather than removing it.
Do not remove privacy and security certification criteria; seek alternative reforms through future rulemaking.
notable_quote: Removing C-CDA functionality without understanding readiness jeopardizes ongoing nationwide interoperability.
